segments.s558-g25: This is a string of rhetorical questions in second person: «چه گویی» and «چه جویی» are elliptical present-tense interrogatives meaning “what do you say?” and “what do you seek?” respectively (here -ی ending is the poetic/present 2sg). «چه» repeats as an interrogative/exclamatory particle. «شاید» is a modal adverb meaning “it is possible / perhaps,” and «بدن» is the noun “body.” Grammatically the verbs are implied in the questions and the clause sets up a hypothetical about what the body can do.
segments.s558-g26: «برین» is a compressed literary form of «بر این» (preposition + demonstrative) = “upon this.” «داستانی» is the noun “tale/story.” «نشاید» is an impersonal, archaic/often poetic negative copula meaning “it is not fitting/ought not to be.” It governs the following infinitive «زدن», here used in the sense of ‘to strike/attack’ (or figuratively ‘to assail/criticize’). Word order is typical poetic Persian: prepositional phrase + noun + impersonal predicate + infinitive.
segments.s558-g27: «روانت‌گر» is a poetic contraction of «روانت اگر» = “if your soul/your spirit.” The clitic «گر» after a pronoun or noun functions like conditional ‘if’ in classical Persian. «از آز» means “from/through greed,” and «فرتوت نیست» is “is not decrepit/aged” (negated predicate). Overall this is a conditional clause: “if your soul is not worn out by greed…”
segments.s558-g28: «نشست» here denotes a resting-place/seat (poetic use) rather than a meeting; «تو» is the second-person pronoun. «جز» is the preposition/particle “except/none but.” «تنگ تابوت» is a genitival phrase “narrow coffin” (adjective before noun in poetry). «نیست» negates/excludes anything else: “there is nothing except the narrow coffin” — i.e. your only place of rest will be the coffin.
segments.s558-g29: «اگر» introduces the conditional “if.” «مرگ دارد» is a present-tense clause “death has” or “if death possesses,” with «دارد» as the present auxiliary/verb. «چنین طبع گرگ» is a noun phrase meaning “such a (wolf‑like) temperament” (چنین = such; طبع = nature/temper; گرگ = wolf) — together: “if death has such a wolfish nature.”
segments.s558-g30: «پر از» is the adjectival phrase “full of.” «می‌یکی» literally “one draught of wine” (می = wine, ‌یکی = one / a single); it qualifies the quantity. «جام» is “cup.” «خواهم» is first-person present/future auxiliary “I will/wish” (poetic present of خواستن), and «بزرگ» is an attributive adjective “large/great.” The normal reading: “I will desire (or take) a large cup full of a single draught of wine.”
segments.s558-g1: This is an adverbial + verbal clause: برو نیز is an archaic/poetic adverbial phrase (here functioning like 'again' / 'once more') modifying the past verb بگذشت. سال دراز (adjective after noun: year + long) is the time-subject/measure phrase. بگذشت is the simple past of گذشت (to pass), so the whole means 'again a long year passed.'
segments.s558-g2: سر تاجور is a noun phrase 'the head/chief of the merchants' (سر = chief). اندر آمد is the past intransitive 'came/entered' where اندر is the locative/introductory preposition used with آمد (classical word order). به + گاز marks the destination/place ('into the gaz').
segments.s558-g3: The clause is elliptical and classical in ordering: یکی پور بودش literally 'one son there-was-to-him' — بودش is بود + enclitic ش marking 'to him/for him', so 'he had one son.' The following دلارام بود is a short clause 'Dolaram was' (an identifying/copular clause giving a name or identity).
segments.s558-g4: ورا نام بهرام بهرام بود is a dative-identifying construction: ورا (to him/for him) + نام (name) + بهرام (the name). The name is then repeated for poetic emphasis, and بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'to him the name Bahram was' → 'his name was Bahram.'
segments.s558-g5: بیاورد is the past tense 'he brought.' و is the coordinating 'and.' بنشاندش is the causative/past 'he seated him' (بنشان + enclitic -ش = 'him'), and زیر تخت is the locative 'under/beneath the throne.' Word order is verb then object then locative, normal in narrative.
segments.s558-g6: بدو گفت is an indirect speech marker: بدو 'to him' (dative) + گفت 'said' (past). کای is the vocative particle 'O,' followed by the epithet سبز شاخ درخت which literally is adjective + head + genitive ('green bough of the tree'); poetic word order places the adjective before the compound noun.
segments.s558-g7: نبودم is the negative past 'I was not' (ن + بودم). فراوان is an adverb/adjective here giving frequency/degree ('much/often'), and من is the emphatic pronoun 'I' (post-verbal emphasis). از تخت شاد is a prepositional phrase literally 'from/of the throne + happy' functioning as a complement meaning 'happy on/at the throne.' The clause as a whole: 'I was not often happy on the throne.'
segments.s558-g8: همه روزگار تو is the subject 'all your days/time'; فرخنده is the predicate adjective 'auspicious/fortunate'; باد is the optative particle 'may' (used to express a wish). This is a conventional blessing: 'May all your days be auspicious.'
segments.s558-g17: تو is the vocative/subject 'you' (poetic). از + چرخ گردان forms a prepositional phrase 'from the turning wheel' (here 'چرخ' = the Wheel of Fortune/fate and گردان is its adjective 'turning/revolving'). مدان is an archaic/poetic negative imperative/prohibitive meaning 'do not suppose/attribute' or 'do not think' and governs the following object phrase 'این ستم' (= 'this injustice/oppression').
segments.s558-g18: چو is a comparative/temporal 'as/like' (here 'like'). از باد literally 'from the wind' functions idiomatically as 'like the wind'. چندی گذاری is a verbal phrase meaning 'you will pass (away) for a short while' where چندی = 'for a little while' and گذاری is a poetic verbal/participial form indicating passing/transition. به دم = 'in a breath/moment' (به as preposition + دم 'moment, breath').
segments.s558-g19: به here means 'for/during' introducing a duration. 'سه سال و سه ماه' lists durations ('three years and three months'). بر سر + 'سه روز' is an idiom meaning 'for (the length of) three days' or 'for three days atop' (بر سر = 'on the head/of the top of' used to mark a time-span).
segments.s558-g20: تهی ماند is a past verbal phrase 'remained empty' (تهی = 'empty/devoid', ماند = 'remained/was left'). زو is the poetic contracted form of از او 'from/of him' (marks that he is absent from it). تخت گیتی فروز is a noun phrase 'the world-illuminating throne' (تخت = 'throne', گیتی = 'world', فروز = 'shining/bright' used adjectivally); the whole means 'the throne of the world, shining, remained empty of him.'
segments.s558-g21: چو = 'when'. The first بهرام is the subject (agent) 'Bahram'. گیتی is the direct object 'the world'. به بهرام is the indirect-object phrase 'to Bahram' (the recipient). داد is the past-tense verb 'gave'. Word order is S O IO V, typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s558-g22: This clause continues with an implied subject (he/Bahram) and the verb داد 'gave'. پسر is the beneficiary/recipient 'the son' (direct object of the provision). مر and ورا are older pronominal/indirect-object markers ('مر' = 'me' or 'for me' in archaic usage; 'ورا' = 'to him/there') and indicate indirect-object relations; the exact distribution is poetic/elliptical but they mark to whom things are given or concerning whom. دخمه آرام is a noun + adjective 'a quiet/peaceful tomb/sepulchre' (دخمه 'tomb', آرام 'quiet/peaceful'). The clause therefore expresses that a peaceful tomb was given for/ to the son (by the same agent).
segments.s558-g23: چنین بود = 'thus/so it was' (چنین 'thus', بود 'was' past). تا بود = 'as long as' or 'while there was' (تا = 'until/while', بود = 'was'). چرخ بلند = 'the lofty/high wheel' (چرخ here = the Wheel of Fortune/fate; بلند = 'lofty/high'). The phrase says that matters remained so while the high Wheel existed.
segments.s558-g24: به انده is a poetic locution: به + انده (participle) = 'having placed/left it so' or 'to that extent'. چه داری is an interrogative construction 'what/why do you have' (چه 'what/why', داری 'you have' present). دلت را = 'your heart' with the enclitic -ت for 'your' and را as the object marker (deltā = 'your heart' as the object). نژند is an adjective 'base/worthless' modifying what is held in the heart; together the clause asks why you harbor something base in your heart given how things stand.
segments.s558-g9: سراینده is a deverbal/agent noun 'poet, singer' used predicatively. باش is the second-person imperative 'be!'; the conjunction و links two parallel predicative phrases, so فزاینده (an adjectival/participle 'increasing, prolific') is another predicate followed by a second imperative باش. In other words: 'Be X and be Y.'
segments.s558-g10: شب و روز is the adverbial phrase 'night and day' (i.e. always). The prefixed prepositional/attitudinal form بآرامش ('with/ in tranquility') and خنده ('laughter/smiling') form a compound adverbial 'with calm and laughter'; the final باش is again the imperative 'be!' governing the whole adverbial phrase. Word order is poetic but reads: 'Night and day, be with tranquility and laughter.'
segments.s558-g11: چنان is the adverb 'so/thus' and رو here functions as an exhortative/imperative 'go/behave' (classical usage). که introduces a result/relative clause 'so that...' and پرسند is a third-person verb form (here subjunctive/optative in sense 'they ask'); روز شمار is a compound 'day-counter' (one who counts days/time). The clause therefore means 'Behave so that the day-counter asks/queries.' Note the poetic inversion of verb and its logical subject.
segments.s558-g12: نپیچی is a negative subjunctive/imperative form 'do not turn' (ن‑ + present subjunctive); سر از is an idiomatic phrase 'turn the head away/from' and is followed by از + noun. شرم پروردگار is a genitive phrase 'the shame/modesty/reverence of the Lord' (meaning reverent fear/modesty before God). Together: 'Do not turn your head away from the Lord's shame/reverence.'
segments.s558-g13: به داد و دهش uses به with two abstract nouns 'داد' (justice) and 'دهش' (giving/generosity) as an instrumental phrase 'by/with justice and giving.' گیتی آباد دار is an instructive/imperative construction: آباد (prosperous) + دار (here functioning verbally as 'keep/make'), so the whole means 'make/keep the world prosperous.' This is a compressed imperatival clause common in classical Persian.
segments.s558-g14: دل زیردستان خود is the object phrase 'the heart(s) of your subordinates' (دل = heart; زیردستان = subordinates; خود = your own/your). شاد دار repeats the imperative construction 'make/keep happy' (شاد = happy; دار = keep/make). The order places the object before the verbal directive, typical of poetic imperatives.
segments.s558-g15: که introduces a clause giving the consequence/result. برکس should be read as بر + کس 'for/ upon anyone'; نماند is the verb 'does not remain/last' (here in the negative sense 'will not endure'); جهان جاودان is the noun phrase 'the eternal world.' Poetic word order places the verb before the noun phrase, yielding: 'that the eternal world does not remain for anyone.'
segments.s558-g16: نه ... و نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor.' Each noun is governed by بر 'on/for' (noting the repeated preposition before تاجدار and موبدان): 'not for the sovereign and not for the priests.' The line denies privilege or permanence for those ranks.

sentences.s558-s13: What do you say, what do you seek, what may the body do? Upon this tale it is not fitting to strike.
sentences.s558-s14: If your soul is not made decrepit by greed, your resting-place will be none other than the narrow coffin.
sentences.s558-s15: If death has such a wolfish temperament, I will want a great cup filled with a single draught of wine.
sentences.s558-s1: Again a long year passed; the chief of the merchants came into the gaz.
sentences.s558-s2: He had one son; Dolaram she was. His name was Bahram — Bahram was (his) name.
sentences.s558-s3: He brought him and seated him beneath the throne; to him he said, 'O green bough of the tree...'
sentences.s558-s4: I was not often joyful upon the throne; may all your days be auspicious.
sentences.s558-s9: Do not ascribe this oppression to the turning Wheel; like the wind you will pass away in a moment.
sentences.s558-s10: For three years and three months, and for three days on its head, the world-illumining throne remained empty of him.
sentences.s558-s11: When Bahram gave the world to Bahram, he gave his son a quiet sepulchre.
sentences.s558-s12: So it was while the lofty Wheel endured; why then do you keep anything base in your heart?
sentences.s558-s5: Be a poet and be prolific; day and night be in tranquility and laughter.
sentences.s558-s6: Act so (be such) that the day-counter may ask; do not turn your head away from the shame/reverence of the Lord.
sentences.s558-s7: By justice and giving keep the world prosperous; keep the hearts of your subordinates joyful.
sentences.s558-s8: So that the eternal world will not remain for anyone — neither for the crowned nor for the mōbeds (priests).
